mappings.rationale: Directly covers the High and Later Middle Ages in western Europe, the primary subject of this section.
Directly relevant to subsections 5 and 7 covering England from the Norman Conquest through the Wars of the Roses, including Scotland, Wales, and Ireland.
Directly relevant to subsection 5.a on the Norman Conquest, introduction of feudalism, and development of royal administration under William I.
Directly covers subsection 2 on the Holy Roman Empire under the Investiture Controversy, Hohenstaufen dynasty, and the Great Interregnum.
Directly relevant to subsection 1.e on the culture of the High Middle Ages, including Latin and vernacular literature.
Directly relevant to subsection 1.e on the intellectual revival, Scholasticism, and developments in philosophy and theology.
Relevant to subsection 4.a on the Habsburg and Luxemburg emperors and the enhancement of Habsburg power and influence in Europe by 1500.
Directly relevant to subsection 5.b.iii on the revolt of the barons under King John and the issuing of Magna Carta.
Relevant to subsection 1.d on the church in medieval society, reform movements including eremitism, Cistercians, and mendicant orders.
Relevant to subsection 1.e on Scholasticism and late medieval intellectual developments in philosophy and theology.
Relevant to subsection 1.a on the early Renaissance in Italy and developments in literature; Dante is the preeminent literary figure of late medieval Italy.
Relevant to subsection 5.c on Italian political developments and the crisis of Florentine republicanism, as well as late medieval political theory.
Relevant to the section's extensive coverage of competing dynasties across Europe — Capetian, Plantagenet, Hohenstaufen, Habsburg, Visconti, Medici, and others.
Relevant to subsection 1.a on the feudal nobility and subsection 2.a on the declining importance of the feudal nobility in the face of changing military technology.
Directly mentioned in subsection 1.c on late medieval intellectual developments including witchcraft.
Directly relevant to subsection 2.b on the Black Death (1347-50) and subsequent epidemics and their economic, social, and political effects.
Relevant to subsection 1.f on the status of Jews in medieval society, their economic role, persecutions, and migrations.
Relevant to subsection 1.f on the status of Jews in medieval European society, including their persecutions and the Inquisition's impact.
Relevant to subsection 3 on the conciliar movement, the Great Schism, and other late medieval reform movements that presaged the Reformation.
Relevant to the section's extensive discussion of the relationship between spiritual and temporal power, papal monarchy, and medieval theological developments.
Relevant to the section's discussion of medieval religious culture and the canonization of Louis IX in 1297.
Relevant to subsection 1.a on the early Italian Renaissance, including the revival of Greek studies and the formation of Humanism and its relationship to Christianity.
Relevant to subsection 8.c on Spanish explorations and territorial acquisitions in the New World and the colonial policy of the Age of Discovery.
Relevant to subsection 8.d on Portuguese development under Prince Henry the Navigator and the establishment of overseas empires.
Relevant to the section's discussion of medieval religious practices and the broader religious context of the crusading movement and medieval Christianity.
Relevant to the section's coverage of late medieval English cultural developments and vernacular literature.
Relevant to subsection 7 on the Slavic and Magyar states, though Russia is treated more fully in section 924.
The section is fundamentally about medieval historiography and the study of the political, social, and cultural institutions of Western Christendom.
